splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate an employer's withdrawal liability from a multiemployer pension plan to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be selected by the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the supervision period beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond each year.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled while a supervisee is a fugitive, allowing courts to find violations and revoke release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to extend the period of supervision beyond the judgment's terms without explicit congressional authorization.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the stricter gatekeeping requirement of § 2244(b)(1)—which categorically bars previously presented claims—when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255, or whether they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, meaning they need only satisfy the requirements of § 2255(h) to bring successive collateral attacks.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines that increase criminal penalties.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's anti-defendant interpretation of ambiguous Guidelines provisions or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant. The Third Circuit held that deference trumps lenity, while the Fourth, Fifth, Sixth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity trumps deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that when a Sentencing Guidelines provision is ambiguous in a criminal context, the rule of lenity requires courts to adopt the interpretation more favorable to the defendant rather than deferring to the Commission's anti-defendant reading.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary takes precedence over the rule of lenity, allowing anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to be met for disqualification) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision means a defendant is disqualified from relief only if all three criminal-history criteria are met, or if meeting any single criterion is disqualifying. This directly determines whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums for many defendants.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its natural conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as effectively meaning 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which expands the definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, is a valid and binding interpretation of the career-offender guideline.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary adding conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in the career-offender guideline is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor, or whether the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses. This split affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders.

positions.label: Commentary is valid — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and that conspiracy and other inchoate offenses qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary is invalid — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and that the commentary impermissibly expands the guideline's scope beyond its plain meaning, so conspiracy convictions cannot serve as career-offender predicates.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state controlled substance is not listed on the federal schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) is limited to substances controlled under federal law or also includes substances controlled only under state law. This split affects the calculation of criminal history and base offense levels for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State-only substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b)'s reference to offenses 'under state law' means any state drug offense qualifies, even if the substance is not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

positions.label: Must be federally controlled substance
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the underlying substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether non-retroactive changes in law can be considered as part of or the sole basis for a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits permit courts to consider such changes, while others hold that non-retroactive changes categorically cannot contribute to a compassionate release finding.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered as one factor, alone or in combination with others, in determining whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes categorically excluded
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, as this would circumvent Congress's decision not to make the change retroactive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for the underlying violations of supervised release at revocation proceedings, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of trust with the court.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible basis for sentences imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit allows courts to punish defendants for the conduct underlying the violation, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences should be based on the breach of the court's trust, not as punishment for the new offense. This affects hundreds of revocation proceedings annually.

positions.label: May punish for underlying violation conduct
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly consider and punish a defendant for the violations of supervised release themselves, not merely for the breach of the court's trust.

positions.label: Revocation based on breach of trust only
positions.summary: Holds that revocation of supervised release sanctions the violation of the court's trust inherent in the defendant's breach of supervised release conditions, and courts may not impose sentences that punish the underlying criminal conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor when converting from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 in good faith.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A) means that property appreciation occurring between the original Chapter 13 filing and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or belongs to the debtor. This split affects the financial outcome for debtors who experience property appreciation while in Chapter 13 before converting.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to estate
positions.summary: Holds that the broad scope of § 541(a) means post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure and § 348(f)(1)(A) establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, consistent with the statute's purpose of protecting good-faith converting debtors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an appeal from the denial of a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees filed in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the 30-day civil deadline) or a criminal matter (subject to the 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, while at least one circuit applies the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case, so the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of sufficiently 'high order' to warrant immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine. Other circuits have not uniformly recognized this as a basis for immediate appeal, creating a potential split on the scope of the collateral order doctrine.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be effectively lost absent immediate review.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are generally not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because the privacy interest can be adequately vindicated through other means such as post-judgment appeal.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: In re City of New York

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's preemption clause, which bars states from enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any ... broker,' preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for personal injury arising from their selection and hiring of motor carriers. The outcome affects liability for the freight brokerage industry nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they are 'related to' the broker's services within the meaning of the FAAAA preemption clause.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or selection of motor carriers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they do not relate to the broker's 'price, route, or service.'

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may be used to address legal errors or constitutional defects in a conviction or sentence that might otherwise be raised in a habeas petition under § 2255. Some circuits allow consideration of such claims as part of the extraordinary and compelling analysis, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for § 2255.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider legal errors or unfairness in the underlying conviction or sentence as one factor in the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used to challenge the validity of a conviction or sentence, as such claims must be raised through § 2255 habeas proceedings.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to state voting rules, including ballot-counting deadlines. The resolution affects how easily pre-election challenges can be brought to voting procedures nationwide and whether such disputes are funneled into post-election litigation.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate has Article III standing to challenge election rules that allegedly affect the validity or counting of ballots in their race, recognizing competitive injury as sufficient.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's generalized competitive interest is insufficient to establish Article III standing to challenge state ballot-counting procedures before an election.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bognet v. Secretary of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
